title: QUIC client-side peer early-data disposition architecture
purpose: Describe how the managed client/runtime path observes peer EncryptedExtensions early-data disposition, surfaces accepted versus rejected disposition, discards dormant ZeroRtt packet-protection material on rejection, and keeps accepted disposition intact without widening public early-data support or claiming anti-replay.
risks: If the parser accepts malformed or duplicated early_data, the client can misclassify peer disposition and leave stale dormant state behind.
If the cleanup is implemented only in the runtime, direct bridge-state callers can retain stale ZeroRtt material after rejection.
If the slice is widened into public early-data admission, it overclaims support instead of staying an internal disposition observation.
design_summary: The TLS transcript-progress path parses EncryptedExtensions and detects the peer early_data extension on the PSK-capable branch. The bridge driver publishes the resulting disposition as a dedicated state update, and the bridge state stores that disposition only after accepted resumption has already been established. When the disposition is rejected, the bridge state reuses the existing key-discard helper to remove only dormant ZeroRtt packet-protection material. Accepted dispositions leave the dormant material intact, and malformed or duplicated early_data data fails closed through the existing fatal/update path.
alternatives_considered: Treat absence of early_data as accepted. Rejected because the requirement needs an explicit rejection boundary that can discard dormant ZeroRtt material.
Clear all packet-protection material on rejection. Rejected because the cleanup must not disturb unrelated handshake or 1-RTT material.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Malformed or duplicated early_data data must fail closed through the fatal/update path.
Rejected dispositions discard only dormant ZeroRtt material.
Accepted dispositions leave dormant ZeroRtt material intact.
The slice does not add 0-RTT receive handling, server admission, key update, transfer, retry, public API widening, or anti-replay support.
